Title: Applications of CRISPR/Cas9 as New Strategies for Short Breeding to Drought Gene in Rice

Plant_Genes.Notes_or_Other_Important_Info: CRISPR/Cas9 editing of OsSAP produced multiple InDel alleles in the first exon, with several homozygous lines already identified in the T0 generation. Across generations, OsSAP expression remained absent while Cas9 expression was stable or weakened, indicating stable inheritance of the edited OsSAP locus even when Cas9 transcripts decreased. The short breeding scheme demonstrates that desired OsSAP genotypes can be fixed early and propagated while maintaining uniform agronomic traits.
Plant_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: OsSAP positively regulates drought tolerance in rice by enhancing ROS-scavenging capacity and stress-responsive gene expression; loss-of-function CRISPR/Cas9 GeD lines show reduced survival, shorter shoots and roots, lower chlorophyll content, fewer tillers, lower 1,000-grain weight, higher H2O2 and MDA accumulation, weaker antioxidant enzyme activities, and lower induction of drought-related transcription factors under drought, whereas OsSAP-T6 overexpression lines show enhanced antioxidant capacity, reduced ROS accumulation, and improved growth and yield under drought.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Mechanism: Under drought, OsSAP promotes the antioxidant defense by increasing activities of SOD, POD, and CAT, leading to reduced accumulation of H2O2 and O2− and lower MDA levels; it also enhances the transcription of multiple stress-responsive genes, which correlates with improved ROS scavenging, delayed leaf senescence, and maintenance of growth and yield under water deficit.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Pathway: Drought stress response and ROS-scavenging/antioxidant defense pathway in rice: OsSAP acts upstream to enhance activities of antioxidant enzymes (SOD, POD, CAT) and to elevate expression of drought-responsive transcription factors (OsABI5, OsRAB16A, OsLEA3, OsLIP9, OsCATA, OsDREB2A, OsNAC5, OsNAC6), thereby improving ROS detoxification, limiting lipid peroxidation, and increasing drought tolerance.
Plant_Genes.Upstream_or_Downstream_Regulation: OsSAP -->(activation)--> OsABI5; OsSAP -->(activation)--> OsRAB16A; OsSAP -->(activation)--> OsLEA3; OsSAP -->(activation)--> OsLIP9; OsSAP -->(activation)--> OsCATA; OsSAP -->(activation)--> OsDREB2A; OsSAP -->(activation)--> OsNAC5; OsSAP -->(activation)--> OsNAC6; OsSAP -->(activation)--> SOD; OsSAP -->(activation)--> POD; OsSAP -->(activation)--> CAT (inferred from higher enzyme activities in OsSAP-T6 and reduced activities in OsSAP GeD lines under drought).
Plant_Genes.Expression_Pattern: RT-PCR showed that OsSAP transcripts were absent in all analyzed OsSAP CRISPR/Cas9 genome-edited plants: OsSAP was not expressed in GeD0 plants, GeD1 plants, or GeD2 seeds, whereas Cas9 remained expressed at varying levels across generations.
Plant_Genes.Key_Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Drought stress imposed by withholding water until soil moisture content reached 20% at the four-leaf stage, followed by 7 days of re-watering for recovery; comparisons also made under well-watered control conditions.
Plant_Genes.Research_Topic: Drought stress tolerance, leaf senescence and ROS-scavenging in rice, and application of CRISPR/Cas9 for rapid breeding of drought-tolerant, high-yield cultivars.
Plant_Genes.Trait_Category: Abiotic stress (drought stress) tolerance; leaf senescence and oxidative stress response; grain yield components (tiller number, 1,000-grain weight).
Plant_Genes.Favorable_Allele: OsSAP-T6 overexpression allele: maintains normal agronomic traits under non-stress conditions while conferring increased drought tolerance, with higher survival rate, greater shoot and root length, higher chlorophyll content, more tillers, and higher 1,000-grain weight compared to Ilmi and OsSAP knockout (GeD) lines under drought.
Plant_Genes.Quantitative_Phenotypic_Alterations: Under drought (20% soil moisture), survival rate of OsSAP-T6 line was 43.5%, whereas GeD0 1-1, GeD0 3-1, and GeD0 3-2 lines had survival rates of 17.4%, 16.8%, and 15.6%, respectively. Shoot lengths were 15.1 cm in OsSAP-T6 vs 6.4, 5.8, and 6.1 cm in GeD0 1-1, GeD0 3-1, and GeD0 3-2. Root lengths were 9.1 cm in OsSAP-T6 vs 2.8, 3.5, and 4.9 cm in the GeD lines. Tiller number under drought was 12.3 in OsSAP-T6 vs 2.3, 2.3, and 2.0 in GeD0 1-1, GeD0 3-1, and GeD0 3-2. 1,000-grain weight under drought was 20.3 g in OsSAP-T6 vs 9.5, 7.8, and 7.1 g in the GeD lines. Under drought, H2O2 content increased by 31.6 ± 0.9% in OsSAP-T6 but by an average of 71.6 ± 4.5% in GeD lines; MDA content increased by 6.5 ± 0.1% in OsSAP-T6 vs 12.6 ± 3.2% in GeD lines. Proline content increased by 494.7 ± 9.1% in OsSAP-T6 vs 283.9 ± 43.9% in GeD lines. SOD, POD, and CAT activities under drought were highest in OsSAP-T6 and lowest in GeD lines.
Plant_Genes.Other_Phenotypic_Effects: Under control conditions, GeD and OsSAP-T6 lines showed no significant differences in survival, growth, tiller number, or 1,000-grain weight compared with Ilmi, indicating that OsSAP manipulation did not markedly affect these traits without drought. DAB and NBT staining revealed extensive brown (H2O2) and blue (O2−) spots in GeD leaves but minimal staining in OsSAP-T6 under drought, consistent with greater ROS accumulation and oxidative damage in OsSAP loss-of-function lines.
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Materials: Oryza sativa ssp. japonica cv. Ilmi (Ilmi) as the wild-type background; CRISPR/Cas9-derived OsSAP genome-edited lines (GeD0 1-1, GeD0 3-1, GeD0 3-2 and related GeD generations); OsSAP-T6 overexpression lines; calli derived from Ilmi for Agrobacterium tumefaciens EHA105-mediated transformation; T0, T1 plants and T2 seeds grown in pots (Wagner pots) and in the field.
Plant_Genes.Variety: Oryza sativa ssp. japonica cv. Ilmi (Ilmi)
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Methods: Design of three OsSAP-targeting sgRNAs (50–70% GC content) and cloning into pRGEB32 CRISPR/Cas9 vector; Agrobacterium-mediated transformation of Ilmi calli; tissue culture and regeneration of T0 plants; PCR and sequencing of target regions to identify T-DNA insertion and OsSAP InDels; RT-PCR/qRT-PCR for OsSAP, Cas9, and drought-related transcription factors using OsActin as control; drought stress imposition by withholding water until 20% soil moisture, followed by recovery; measurement of survival rate, shoot length, root length, chlorophyll content, tiller number, and 1,000-grain weight; DAB staining and quantitative peroxide assay for H2O2; NBT staining for O2−; assays of proline, MDA, SOD, POD, and CAT activities; statistical analysis by one-way ANOVA and Duncan's multiple range test.
Plant_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9-mediated targeted knockout of OsSAP (multiple homozygous GeD lines), use of OsSAP-T6 overexpression lines, drought stress treatment at 20% soil moisture, phenotyping of survival, shoot/root length, chlorophyll content, tiller number and 1,000-grain weight, biochemical assays for H2O2, MDA, proline, SOD, POD and CAT activities, DAB and NBT staining for ROS visualization, and RT/qRT-PCR analysis of OsSAP, Cas9, and drought-responsive transcription factors.
Plant_Genes.Field_Trials: Ear-to-row field planting of T1 plants derived from selected OsSAP genome-edited T0 plants (each spike assigned an accession number, one row per line) with bulk harvest of T2 seeds; agronomic traits (heading date, plant height, culm length in T0 and T1 plants; grain length, grain width, and 1,000-grain weight in T1 and T2 seeds) were evaluated, with coefficients of variation for T1 plants and T2 seeds below 5%.
Plant_Genes.Gene_Discovery_or_Cloning_Method: Reverse genetics: previously identified OsSAP (AB734097) was functionally characterized by CRISPR/Cas9-mediated targeted mutagenesis in the first exon using three sgRNAs in the japonica cultivar Ilmi, generating homozygous genome-edited (GeD) lines; existing OsSAP-T6 overexpression lines were also used for comparison.
Plant_Genes.Breeding_Application_Value: OsSAP is a strong candidate gene for breeding drought-tolerant, high-yield rice. Overexpression of OsSAP improves drought resistance while maintaining normal agronomic performance under non-stress conditions and increasing key yield components under drought, making it suitable for cultivar development without yield penalties. CRISPR/Cas9-mediated editing can generate homozygous OsSAP alleles already in T0 plants, and the ear-to-row field strategy with low coefficients of variation in T1 plants and T2 seeds (<5%) demonstrates that OsSAP-targeted editing can be efficiently integrated into short breeding cycles for rapid development of drought-tolerant rice varieties.
